steps.title: Spot the defining trigger
steps.content: The claim is that sounds are “directed primarily at predators” rather than for same-species communication. The key distinction is what triggers the sounds. A predator-defense sound is specifically provoked by and responsive to predator threat, then ceases when the threat passes. A communication signal would persist independent of attacks.

steps.title: Test the trigger-response pattern
steps.content: Choice A demonstrates this exact pattern. Caterpillars emit sounds only during the simulated attacks and nearly all stop within seconds after—a stimulus-response sequence diagnostic of predator defense. This proves the sounds are not ongoing communication with conspecifics, since they don't appear in non-attack contexts. The other choices describe sound variation or species differences without addressing whether sounds are triggered by predator threat. That matches choice A — A is correct.
